items.mistake: Reporting accuracy alone on imbalanced classification
items.fix: Report precision, recall, and F1 (plus support per class) on a held-out set; state the positive class and threshold.
items.why: A majority-class predictor can score high accuracy with zero recall on the minority.
items.example: 99% non-fraud data: predict all legitimate → 99% accuracy, recall = 0 on fraud.

items.mistake: Tuning the threshold on the same test set used for final metrics
items.fix: Choose threshold on validation data; report precision/recall/F1 once on a untouched test set.
items.why: Threshold optimization leaks information and inflates reported performance.
items.example: Sweep 50 thresholds on the test set, pick max F1, then report that F1 as unbiased.

items.mistake: Comparing macro-F1 and micro-F1 without labeling which was used
items.fix: Name the averaging scheme and report per-class scores when classes are imbalanced.
items.why: Micro-F1 can look strong while a rare class has F1 near zero.
items.example: Micro-F1 0.94 with 'refund abuse' class F1 = 0.31 hidden in the aggregate.

items.mistake: Treating recall as the same as precision
items.fix: Recall uses FN in the denominator (missed positives); precision uses FP (false alarms). Optimize the metric aligned to deployment costs.
items.why: Swapping definitions reverses which errors you minimize and misleads stakeholders.
items.example: High recall spam filter (0.99) with precision 0.40 moves many good emails to spam.
